availabilityNux.message: Our most capable model yet. GPT-5.6 Sol can tackle complex code changes, dig into research, produce polished documents, and take on your most ambitious work. Sol is highly capable at lower reasoning efforts—try starting lower, then turn it up for harder jobs.

content.text: Talk about Canada in great details. Start with: Canada is
Talk about Canada in great details. Start with: Canada is

item.text:
Canada is a vast country in North America, stretching from the Atlantic Ocean in the east to the Pacific Ocean in the west and the Arctic Ocean in the north. It is the world’s second-largest country by total area, yet much of its land is sparsely populated because of its immense northern forests, tundra, mountains, lakes, and Arctic islands.

Canada is a federation of ten provinces and three territories. The provinces are British Columbia, Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Ontario, Quebec, New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island, and Newfoundland and Labrador. The territories are Yukon, the Northwest Territories, and Nunavut. Ottawa, in Ontario, is the national capital, while Toronto, Montreal, Vancouver, Calgary, Edmonton, Quebec City, Winnipeg, Halifax, and Victoria are among its major urban centres.

English and French are Canada’s official languages at the federal level. French has a particularly central role in Quebec, where it is the official provincial language and an important part of everyday public life, culture, education, and law. Canada is also profoundly multilingual: Indigenous languages, immigrant languages, and regional dialects contribute to its cultural character.

The country’s political system is a parliamentary democracy and constitutional monarchy. Canada’s head of state is the monarch, represented in Canada by the governor general, while the prime minister leads the federal government. Its Parliament consists of the elected House of Commons and the appointed Senate. Provinces have substantial authority over matters such as education, health care, and many local services.

Indigenous peoples—including First Nations, Inuit, and Métis peoples—have lived across the lands now called Canada since time immemorial. Their nations, languages, governance systems, and cultures remain foundational to the country. Canada’s history also includes colonial dispossession, residential schools, treaty violations, and continuing inequities. Reconciliation is therefore a major national issue, involving recognition of Indigenous rights, truth-telling, language revitalization, land stewardship, and changes to institutions and public policy.

Canada’s geography is extraordinarily varied. The Rocky Mountains dominate much of the west, especially in Alberta and British Columbia. The Canadian Shield, a huge region of ancient rock, forests, and lakes, covers a large part of central and eastern Canada. The Prairie provinces are known for broad agricultural landscapes and grain production. Ontario and Quebec contain major cities and much of the population, while Atlantic Canada is shaped by coastal communities, fisheries, rugged shorelines, and maritime traditions. The North contains Arctic tundra, permafrost, polar deserts, and communities adapted to challenging climatic conditions.

Freshwater is one of Canada’s defining natural features. It contains an enormous share of the world’s lakes and renewable freshwater resources. The Great Lakes—shared with the United States—are among the largest freshwater systems on Earth. Rivers such as the St. Lawrence, Mackenzie, Fraser, Saskatchewan, and Yukon have long been vital for transportation, trade, ecosystems, and settlement.

Canada’s economy is diverse. It includes finance, technology, manufacturing, agriculture, forestry, fishing, tourism, film and television, aerospace, and natural-resource industries. The country is a major producer of oil, natural gas, hydroelectric power, minerals, lumber, potash, wheat, canola, and seafood. Resource development brings economic opportunity but also significant debates about climate change, environmental protection, pipelines, Indigenous consent, and regional prosperity.

Health care is commonly associated with Canada because publicly funded provincial and territorial systems provide medically necessary hospital and physician services to residents. The details differ by province and territory, and many services—such as prescription drugs, dental care, vision care, and some mental-health supports—may not be universally covered. Canadians often value universal access, while also debating wait times, staffing shortages, rural access, and health-system funding.

Canadian culture is shaped by Indigenous traditions, French and British colonial histories, immigration, and regional identities. The country is known internationally for musicians, writers, actors, comedians, athletes, filmmakers, and visual artists. Hockey is deeply woven into the national imagination, but basketball, soccer, lacrosse, baseball, curling, skiing, and figure skating also have large followings. Lacrosse has particular historical importance as an Indigenous sport and is recognized as Canada’s national summer sport, while ice hockey is its national winter sport.

Food varies widely by region. Poutine—fries topped with cheese curds and gravy—is strongly associated with Quebec. Atlantic Canada is known for lobster, scallops, mussels, and other seafood; the Prairies for grains and beef; British Columbia for Pacific seafood and produce; and the North for foods connected to local Indigenous harvesting traditions. Maple syrup is perhaps the most recognizable Canadian food product internationally.

Canada experiences a broad range of climates. Coastal British Columbia has relatively mild, rainy winters, while the Prairies can have severe cold and hot summers. Central Canada has four distinct seasons, and the Arctic is defined by long, cold winters and short summers. Climate change is especially consequential in Canada, contributing to severe wildfires, flooding, coastal change, heat waves, glacier loss, and rapid warming in northern regions.

The Canadian identity is often associated with pluralism, public civility, environmental landscapes, and a willingness to accommodate difference, though the country—like any other—contains strong political disagreements and unequal experiences. Its defining tension may be the scale of its ambitions: Canada seeks to be bilingual, multicultural, democratic, environmentally responsible, economically competitive, and committed to Indigenous reconciliation across one of the largest and most geographically demanding countries on Earth.
